title: Chapter Eight: Sleepwalk
content: Cold.
That's what comes first. Cold from below. Cold seeping up through the soles of my feet into the bones of me, into the marrow, and I don't understand it because I was in bed, I was—
Where am I?

My eyes are open. They've been open. I'm standing in a corridor I don't recognize and my hand is on a doorknob. Brass. Tarnished. Cold like the floor. My fingers are wrapped around it and I don't remember reaching for it, don't remember walking here, don't remember—

The last thing. The last thing I remember is the ceiling. My ceiling. The water stain shaped like a hand. I was watching it at midnight, maybe after, watching and thinking about the tower, about the photograph frame, about Peter, and then—
Now.

4:03 AM on my phone screen. The light blinding. East wing. I know it's the east wing because of the windows, because the lake is out there somewhere, black and vast, and the corridor stretches in both directions like a throat.
I'm barefoot. Pajamas. No cardigan. The cold is unbearable and I'm standing outside a room I have never entered and my hand is on the doorknob like it knows where it's going.
My hand.
I peel my fingers back one by one. They resist. Like they want to turn it. Like they've turned it before.
"Hello, Maeve."
I spin. My heel catches on the cold stone and I nearly go down, grabbing the wall, and Flora is sitting on the floor twelve feet away, cross-legged, like she's been waiting.
Like she's been *waiting*.
"How long have you been there?"

She tilts her head. That gesture I've seen a dozen times now, the one I thought was performance, the little-girl-lost tilt that makes her look twelve instead of twenty-three. But this time it's different. This time her face does something I haven't seen before.
It shakes.

Not her head. Her *face*. The expression trembles, like it can't hold itself together, like the mask is cracking from the inside. Her eyes are red. Not crying-red. Awake-red. The red of someone who's been staring at something for hours and can't look away.
"Flora. How long."
"Long enough."
"That's not an answer."

"You were talking to him." Her voice is flat. No melody in it. She always speaks in melody, in lilt, in the music of someone who knows how pretty she sounds. Not now. Now she sounds tired and raw and her chin is pressed to her knees and she looks small, she looks actually small, not the performed smallness she wears like perfume. "You called him by name."
The cold in my feet spreads. Up through my shins. Into my stomach.
"Who."
"You know who." She won't look at me. Her eyes are fixed on the doorknob I just released. "Peter. You said you knew him. You said—" She stops. Swallows. "You said you'd been waiting for him."
The corridor is very quiet. The old-pipe hum of the manor, the distant settle of timber, and underneath it something else — the absence of sound where my breathing should be. I've stopped breathing.
"I don't know any Peter."

"You said his name. You walked right past me like I wasn't here and you said, *Peter, I've been waiting for you.* And then you came here." She looks up at last and her eyes are huge and frightened and I realize this is what Flora looks like when she's not trying to control what I see. "You came to this room. You tried the handle. It's locked. You stood here for forty minutes trying the handle over and over."
Forty minutes.
I was standing here for forty minutes with my hand on a locked doorknob, and I don't remember a single second.
"I was asleep."
"Yes."
"I was *asleep*."

"I know." She unfolds from the floor. Stands. She's shorter than me but right now the shadow she casts in the emergency-light glow seems to fill the whole corridor. "Maeve. What did they lock you up for?"
The question hits like a slap. "Excuse me?"
"Before. The institution. What did they—"
"That's none of your business."
"Nothing is any of my business." She steps closer. "That's how we survive here. Not-our-business. But you've made it my business, walking around in the dark saying names you shouldn't know."
"I don't know that name."
"You said it."
"I was *asleep*."
"That's worse." She whispers it. "That's so much worse."

She turns and walks away. Her footsteps echo, bare feet on stone, and she doesn't look back, and the corridor swallows her and I'm left standing outside a locked room I've never entered with my hand still tingling from the doorknob and the word *Peter* in my mouth like something I've been choking on for years without knowing.

I go back to my room. I don't run. I want to run but my feet know the floor plan better than I do — they navigate the hallways without consulting me, and isn't that just perfect, isn't that exactly what I need, my body moving through this house like it has its own agenda.
The laptop is where I left it. Still recording.
I sit. I pull up the footage. I scroll to midnight. There I am, lying in bed, watching the ceiling. My eyes are open. I'm not moving. I watch myself not-move for an hour and twelve minutes.
At 1:15 AM, I sit up.
Not waking up. Not the groggy fumble of interrupted sleep. I sit up like a string pulls me vertical. My eyes are open. My face is—

Slack. That's the only word for it. Slack like the muscles have forgotten they're supposed to hold expression. My mouth hangs slightly open. My eyes stare at nothing, or something I can't see, and they don't blink. They don't *blink*.
I watch myself swing my legs out of bed. Stand. Walk out of frame.
The bed stays empty for three hours.
At 3:52 AM, I walk back into frame. Lie down. Close my eyes.

Three hours. Nearly three hours of lost time, and my brain has nothing — not a flicker, not a smear, not so much as a temperature gradient. Just blank. Just gone. Like someone carved those hours out of my head with a spoon.
I set the laptop to record again. Then I prop my phone on the dresser, angled toward the bedroom door. Hidden behind the water glass. Wide lens. If I get up again, it'll catch which way I go.
If.
When.
I know it's when.

The next night I don't sleep. Can't. I lie in the dark with my eyes fixed on the water-stain ceiling and I wait for the string to pull me upright, but it doesn't come. My body stays put. By dawn I'm exhausted and shaking and I almost believe last night was a fluke, a one-time rupture in the order of things.
Almost.
I check the footage anyway.
I haven't moved. The laptop shows eight hours of motionless bed. The phone shows the door, closed, unmoved. I was here all night. I was *here*.
Relief hits so hard it makes me dizzy.
Then I notice the timestamps.

The laptop and the phone are two minutes out of sync. The phone is two minutes ahead. I never noticed. Never thought to check. And last night, the night I walked — the phone was set to overwrite after six hours. I checked it this morning and it was blank. Automatically wiped. I assumed it hadn't captured anything because I'd set it up wrong.
But what if it did capture something? What if it recorded exactly where I went and what I did, and then it erased itself, and I'll never know?
Two minutes. An entire corridor of uncertainty in two minutes.

I reset the phone. I set it to manual delete. I check it three times. I hide it behind the lamp this time, not the water glass, and I angle it wider and I test the low-light recording and it works, it captures, and I lie down and I close my eyes and I *sleep*.
I wake standing at the window.

Not my window. The landing window, the one that overlooks the east garden and the lake beyond. My palm is flat against the glass and it's cold, so cold, and my breath has fogged the pane and I can see the lake in the mist-dark before dawn and I have no idea how I got here.

My room is down the hall. I'm barefoot again. The stone has leached all the warmth from my feet and they ache, they throb, I must have been standing here for—how long? Long enough for the condensation to make a hand-print around my palm.
I go back to my room. I don't let myself think. Thinking is a privilege and I haven't earned it.
The laptop. Last night's footage. There I am at 12:48 AM, sitting up. Slack face. Open eyes. Walking out.
The phone. I grab it. My hands are shaking so badly I nearly drop it twice. I find the file. I press play.
And I watch myself walk.

The footage is grainy, low-light, black-and-white from the night mode. But it's me. It's my body moving down the corridor with a gait I don't recognize — not my walk, not my shoulders, something straighter, something *older*. My arms hang at my sides but my hands are flexing, opening and closing, like they're remembering how to grip.
I open doors.

One after another. Guest rooms. Linen closets. The study. I push each door open, stand in the threshold, and speak. The phone's microphone is too far to catch words, but I can see my mouth moving, and the rhythm is wrong — too slow, too deliberate, like I'm reciting something learned by heart.
I close each door and move to the next.
And then the window.
The landing window. I stand there and my reflection looks back from the glass and—
No.

I rewind. I watch it again. I press my finger to the screen and I trace the outline of the figure in the glass and it's not right. The posture is wrong — shoulders back, chin up, a confidence I have never in my life possessed. And the face, the face reflected in the glass, catches the last of the moonlight and for two frames, three, it's—
Not mine.

I don't mean I look different in moonlight. I mean the bone structure is wrong. The jaw is wider. The eyebrows sit lower. There's a certainty in the expression that isn't mine, has never been mine, and the reflection holds for three seconds and then my body turns away and the glass is empty.
I'm gripping the phone so hard the case creaks.
Keep watching. Keep watching.
My body moves down the stairs. Into the drawing room. Past the sofas, past the fireplace, to the piano.
The piano.
I sit on the bench. I lift my hands. And I play.

Not hunting-and-pecking. Not a child's melody. My fingers move across the keys with a precision that makes my stomach lurch, and I can hear it now, faint and tinny through the phone's speaker — Debussy, *Clair de Lune*, and it's beautiful. It's *beautiful* and I can't play piano. I took lessons for six months when I was nine and I quit because my hands were too small for the octaves and I never touched a keyboard again.
I watch my hands play. My *sleep* hands. They know exactly where to go.
I play for four minutes and twelve seconds. Then I stop. I lower the keyboard cover. I walk back upstairs. I get into bed. I close my eyes.
The footage ends.

I am sitting on my bed with my phone in my lap and the morning is coming grey through the window and I can still smell it — piano wood. Old wood, old varnish, the particular mustiness of hammers and strings and a hundred years of someone else's music. I can smell it on my *fingers*.
I lift my hands to my face. I smell them. Polish. Rosin. The faint metallic tang of copper-wound strings.
My fingers remember the keys. The muscle memory is there, lodged in the tendons, and when I flex them I can almost feel the ivory under my fingertips. The weight of the action. The give of the keys.
This is not my memory.
This is not my body anymore.
Two possibilities.

One: the twins are drugging me. The tea every evening, the chamomile that tastes faintly of something else, something green and bitter underneath the honey. They're dosing me and I'm sleeping deeper than I should, and one or both of them is guiding me through the house, scripting me, making me walk and talk and play pianos I can't play. A performance for their benefit. Or for mine. Gaslight, gaslight, gaslight — the oldest trick in the book, and I know it because I've read about it in the institution library, read about it in the Reddit threads, and isn't it convenient that I know the word for exactly what's happening to me? Isn't it convenient that I have a framework that makes me the victim?
Two: I'm losing my mind.

Just — losing it. The way I lost it before, when I was seventeen, when the world folded in on itself and they put me in a room with a mattress on the floor and told me I was safe. I was safe from *myself*. And I got better. I got *better*. I built my narrative, my evidence, my rational observer's stance, and I became someone who reports on haunting instead of being haunted, and it worked, it *worked*, for six years it worked.
But what if it wasn't gone? What if it was just — sleeping? What if something in me has been waiting, the way I apparently told Flora I was waiting for Peter, and now the manor has woken it up?
Either the twins are doing this to me. Or I'm doing it to myself.
Either way, I can't trust myself.

I've been the narrator of this story — my story — for six years. I've been the one who watches, who records, who tells the truth because the truth is the only weapon I have. But what if my truth is a lie? What if the camera lies and my memory lies and the person typing these words is the least reliable narrator in the room?
The Reddit app is open. I don't remember opening it. My thumbs are on the keyboard and the update field is blank and the cursor blinks and I type:
*I don't think the ghosts are the problem. I think I am.*
My thumb hovers over Post.

If I post this, I'm committed. I'm on record. Six years of rational reporting, of measured analysis, of being the one who doesn't jump to conclusions — and this is what I have to show for it. The woman who walked. The woman who played. The woman who called a dead man's name in the dark and said she'd been waiting for him.
The institution was right to lock me up.

The thought comes from somewhere deep and black and I can't reach it to strangle it. It just sits there, true as gravity, and I can't tell if it's the most honest thing I've ever thought or the most pathological.
My thumb is still hovering.
I press Post.

The little whoosh sound. Sent. Published. Irrevocable. And the relief I expect — the satisfaction of committing to a truth, even a terrible one — doesn't come. Just hollowness. Just the grey morning and the smell of piano wood on my fingers and the sense that I've just handed someone a weapon.
I don't know who.
The floorboard creaks outside my door. I look up.
The doorway is empty. But the shadow that pulls away from the frame — quick, retreating — is the wrong height for Flora. Too tall. Too still.
Miles.
Watching. Listening. Saying nothing.
His silence is worse than any question he could ask. Because silence means he already knows the answer. Silence means I'm not the only one who's been paying attention.

I sit on my bed with my broken narrative and my stolen muscle memory and my posted confession, and I wait for the next night, because I know — with the hollow certainty of someone who no longer trusts her own mind — that I will walk again.
And whatever walks in me when I sleep is getting stronger.
